skill.name: creating-commands
skill.description: Helps users design, create, and review Claude Code slash commands with templates, frontmatter guidance, argument patterns, and validation checklists.
skill.summary: Create and review Claude Code slash commands using structured templates and documented best practices.
skill.author: BrendanShields

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and filesystem patterns, but manual review shows they are mostly markdown examples for Claude Code slash command authoring. No malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or real cryptographic code was found. The remaining risk is medium because the skill encourages creation of reusable commands that may include broad Bash permissions.

medium_findings.title: Broad Shell Tool Permission Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes example slash command frontmatter that grants broad shell access such as Bash(bash:*) or Bash(npm:*). This is legitimate command-authoring guidance, but generated commands using these examples can run external commands if the user accepts them without narrowing permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context confirms these are documented Claude Code allowed-tools examples, not hidden execution code. The risk is real when generated commands preserve broad Bash permissions.

medium_findings.title: Command Files Written to Claude Command Directories
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to save generated commands under .claude/commands/ or ~/.claude/commands/. This is expected behavior for slash commands, but commands placed there may become reusable automation with tool permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly document slash command storage locations. This is a legitimate filesystem use, with moderate risk because saved commands can affect later agent behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many lines as weak cryptography, but the reviewed files contain markdown command examples, descriptions, model names, and validation text. No cryptographic algorithm use, credential handling, or security bypass code was found in the cited contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of representative flagged lines found markdown documentation and template frontmatter only. There is no evidence of hashing, encryption, or weak cryptographic implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Shell Tool Permission Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes example slash command frontmatter that grants broad shell access such as Bash(bash:*) or Bash(npm:*). This is legitimate command-authoring guidance, but generated commands using these examples can run external commands if the user accepts them without narrowing permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line context confirms these are documented Claude Code allowed-tools examples, not hidden execution code. The risk is real when generated commands preserve broad Bash permissions.

content.value_statement: Custom slash commands are useful but easy to structure poorly. This skill guides command design with templates, arguments, frontmatter, and validation checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through purpose, arguments, and command scope before creating a command.
Selects basic, argument-based, or workflow templates for different slash command needs.
Explains Claude Code frontmatter fields such as description, allowed-tools, argument-hint, and model.
Shows how to use positional arguments, full argument strings, and file references in prompts.
Provides validation checklists and anti-patterns for reviewing existing command files.
limitations: It provides markdown guidance and templates, not a standalone command registry or installer.
Generated commands still require user review before saving or running.
Broad allowed-tools examples may need narrowing for sensitive projects.
It focuses on Claude Code slash commands, not general shell script development.

use_cases.title: Create Team Project Commands
use_cases.description: Define reusable project commands that live in .claude/commands/ and follow shared naming and frontmatter rules.

use_cases.title: Build Personal Automation Shortcuts
use_cases.description: Create personal commands for repeated coding tasks, reviews, tests, or issue workflows in a local Claude Code setup.

use_cases.title: Review Existing Slash Commands
use_cases.description: Check existing command files for unclear descriptions, missing argument hints, broad tool permissions, and weak prompt structure.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code slash command that runs our test suite and summarizes failures. It should not require arguments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Command With Arguments
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a slash command that takes an issue number as input, fetches the issue from GitHub, and guides the fix workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Existing Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Claude Code slash command for naming, frontmatter, arguments, allowed tools, and prompt clarity. Suggest specific improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Workflow Command
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced command using skills or agents
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow slash command that uses a specialist skill or analysis agent, accepts a target path, and reports actionable findings.

output_examples.input: Create a command that checks code quality.
output_examples.output: A named command concept with a clear description, restricted tool guidance, ordered execution steps, and a validation checklist.

output_examples.input: Review a command that accepts an issue number.
output: Findings about argument handling and frontmatter clarity.
Suggested changes to allowed tools and command naming.
A concise final checklist before saving the command.

output_examples.output: A workflow outline covering tests, build checks, deployment steps, verification, and safer tool permission recommendations.

best_practices: Use the narrowest allowed-tools entry that supports the command purpose.
Write descriptions that explain what the command does and when to use it.
Validate names, arguments, and prompt steps before saving commands.
anti_patterns: Do not copy broad Bash permissions into commands that only need one tool.
Do not create vague commands with unclear descriptions or missing argument hints.
Do not hardcode project-specific paths when arguments would make the command reusable.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code slash command markdown files with clear prompts, frontmatter, and argument patterns.

faq.question: Can it review existing commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare existing command files against naming, argument, frontmatter, and prompt quality guidance.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands itself?
faq.answer: No evidence found that the skill runs commands itself. It documents command templates that may grant tool access.

faq.question: Why is there a medium security rating?
faq.answer: The skill teaches reusable command creation and includes broad Bash permission examples that users should narrow before use.

faq.question: Where are commands saved?
faq.answer: The guidance references project commands under .claude/commands/ and personal commands under ~/.claude/commands/.

faq.question: Is it useful outside Claude Code?
faq.answer: Its guidance is specific to Claude Code slash commands, though prompt structure ideas may help other AI coding tools.
